summary: I served in the United States Army starting December 15, 1965. My combat duty was mostly in Vietnam, and I was trained like an officer during boot camp. The Army chose me, and I did my best to obey and listen to my instructors throughout my service. It was a tough experience but one that shaped my life deeply.

I served in the Vietnam War, specifically from 1966 through 1973, mostly in the An Khe area. I was part of the 1st Cavalry Division and saw combat, including taking and defending hills against the enemy. The war was brutal and left lasting scars on me and many others. Despite the hardships, I did my duty and witnessed the ongoing struggles of soldiers throughout those years.

My combat duty was stationed at An Khe in the North Highlands of Vietnam. I arrived there with the 1st Cavalry Division and experienced intense combat situations. An Khe was a tough place, and it was there I carried out my duties delivering ammo and supporting infantry operations. It became my home base during my time in Vietnam.

Before joining the Army, I was living in Sacramento, California. It was from there that I was drafted into the United States Army in December 1965. Sacramento was my home before I went off to boot camp and eventually to Vietnam. It was a big change going from there to the battlefields of Southeast Asia.

I was assigned to the 1st Cavalry Division during my service in Vietnam. We operated mainly around An Khe and took part in many combat missions, including helicopter deployments to places like Cambodia and no man's land. The division was well-known, and I even remember the voice of Garry Owens associated with it. Being part of this unit was a defining part of my military experience.

During my time in Vietnam, I traveled with infantry units by helicopter to Cambodia and other locations. These missions were dangerous and often involved going into no man's land. Cambodia was one of the places where our troops operated, and I sometimes didn't even know exactly where I was. It was part of the broader conflict zone we were engaged in.

I never received the Purple Heart, even though I feel I should have. I got several medals, including the Vietnamese Campaign Medal and others, but the Purple Heart and infantry badge eluded me. I think I came home feeling almost naked without those honors. It’s something that has stuck with me over the years.

The USO was present during my time in Vietnam and provided some entertainment for the troops. Although space was limited and sometimes entertainment was stopped due to rowdiness, we still had some shows and music to lift our spirits. I even participated in skits and singing with fellow soldiers to keep morale up. The USO was a welcome break from the hardships of war.

Before going to Vietnam, I trained at Fort Benning, Georgia, for boot camp. It was part of my preparation for combat duty, and the training was intense and thorough. Fort Benning was where I learned many of the skills I would later use in the field. It was an important step in my military journey.

I also spent time training at Fort Sill, Oklahoma, where I focused on artillery. This was part of my military training before deployment to Vietnam. Fort Sill helped me understand the use of weapons like the 105 Howitzers, which I later helped deliver in combat. It was a key part of my artillery and infantry support training.

China Beach was one of the places I traveled to during my service, although I did not elaborate much on my experiences there. It was part of the broader Vietnam theater where soldiers like me operated. The name China Beach is familiar as a location connected to the war effort and troop movements.

Cam Ranh Bay was another location I visited while serving in Vietnam. It was a significant base area used by the military during the war. Like China Beach, it was part of the network of places where we operated and received supplies. My time there was part of the overall experience of being deployed in Vietnam.

I also traveled to Da Nang during my service in Vietnam. Da Nang was an important city and military hub during the war. It was one of the places where troops like me were stationed or passed through. My experience there was part of the broader combat and support operations in the region.

The Philippines was another place I traveled to during my military service, though I did not provide many details about my time there. It was part of the broader area of operations and training before or after Vietnam. The Philippines was a familiar location in the context of my service journey.

I was awarded the Vietnamese Campaign Medal along with other medals like the defense medal and a sharpshooter medal. I actually received these medals by mail about ten to twelve years after the war when I sent in for them. The Vietnamese Campaign Medal was a recognition of my service during the conflict in Vietnam.
cues: ten to twelve years after service
